dimensions.summary: This is genuinely good code — the kind a senior engineer would accept with only minor comments. The IndexedDB layer (src/db.ts) is correct and idiomatic: stores are created only inside onupgradeneeded keyed on DB_VERSION, the v1→v2 bump is purely additive (guarded by objectStoreNames.contains), every mutation is a read-modify-write inside ONE readwrite transaction with full error/abort wiring and db.close() in finally, and the add()-vs-put() distinction (reject-on-create vs upsert-on-update) is deliberate and right. The date logic (src/status.ts, src/season.ts) — the part the ACs hinge on — is correct: addDays uses local-component construction (new Date(y, m-1, d) + setDate), deliberately avoiding the new Date("YYYY-MM-DD") UTC-parse off-by-one, and status comparison uses lexicographic compare of zero-padded ISO strings (chronologically valid, no residual Date). season.ts is pure integer branching over month 1-12 with no timezone surface. Validation is parse-don't-trust at one shared boundary (validatePlantFields) reused by both add and edit. Strict TS (plus noUnused*, noImplicitReturns) passes clean; 43/43 tests pass and genuinely drive real entry points (real DOM clicks, real IndexedDB writes, re-opened-store read-back, mutation/negative probes). The real deductions: (1) a shipped-but-dead data path — getHemisphere exists with no setter and no UI control, so hemisphere is permanently "north" for any real user (southern-hemisphere users get inverted seasons with no recourse); (2) very heavy, near-spec-prose doc comments that will rot; (3) a duplicate test case and minor selector-escaping asymmetry. Nothing here is a correctness bug. Grade: A-.

findings.detail: IndexedDB layer is textbook-correct. Schema changes happen ONLY inside onupgradeneeded via upgradeSchema(), keyed on DB_VERSION, and the v1->v2 bump is purely additive — each createObjectStore is guarded by !db.objectStoreNames.contains(...), so a fresh open creates both stores and an existing v1 DB gains only app-settings with plants and its records untouched. This is the correct migration discipline for the modality.
findings.evidence: src/db.ts:51-60 (upgradeSchema), :63-71 (openDb)

findings.detail: Mutations are transactionally correct. markPlantWatered/updatePlantFields do get->mutate->put inside a SINGLE readwrite transaction (no read-after-write race), write back the FULL merged record (so a partial put can't drop fields), reject on missing id rather than put a blank/partial record, and wire req.onerror + tx.onerror + tx.onabort with db.close() in finally. The mutation test (partial put -> AC-7 fails) the evaluator ran confirms this is load-bearing, not decorative.
findings.evidence: src/db.ts:112-144 (markPlantWatered), :166-202 (updatePlantFields), :218-236 (deletePlant)

findings.detail: add() vs put() is a deliberate, correct distinction: addPlant uses store.add() so a duplicate id rejects loudly on create, while the updaters use put() for intentional upsert-of-existing. deletePlant correctly uses store.delete(id) (idempotent no-op on missing key), never store.clear(). Senior-level attention to the IndexedDB API contract.

The date/season logic the ACs hinge on is genuinely correct. addDays parses to integer y/m/d and builds a LOCAL-time date via new Date(year, month-1, day) + setDate(getDate()+days) — correctly avoiding the new Date("YYYY-MM-DD") UTC-midnight parse that off-by-ones in negative-UTC zones — then re-extracts zero-padded local y/m/d. computeStatus compares zero-padded ISO strings lexicographically, which equals chronological order with no surviving Date object. season.ts takes a numeric month (1-12) and is pure integer branching: zero timezone surface. todayLocalDate()/currentLocalMonth() use getFullYear/getMonth/getDate (local), consistently.
findings.evidence: src/status.ts:31-40 (addDays), :54-67 (computeStatus); src/season.ts:41-50 (seasonForMonth); src/plant.ts:38-44; src/main.ts:221-223

findings.detail: Single parse-don't-trust boundary. validatePlantFields (trim name, Number.isInteger && >0 intervals, throwing typed InvalidPlantInputError) is shared by BOTH createPlant (add) and handleEditSubmit (edit), so neither path can write a blank name or NaN/<=0 interval. UI catches InvalidPlantInputError to show a message but re-throws all other errors so DEV failures aren't silently swallowed — a good, consistent error policy across handleSubmit/handleWaterClick/handleDeleteConfirm/handleEditSubmit.

Event delegation is the right structural choice for re-rendered controls: one click listener + one submit listener on the list container handle every per-plant water/edit/delete/confirm/cancel button via target.closest(...) and the data-plant-id seam, so re-rendering (container.innerHTML = '' then rebuild) never leaves stale per-button listeners. The destructive delete is correctly two-step (button reveals a confirm row; only the confirm control writes).
findings.evidence: src/main.ts:255-330 (delegated click), :334-346 (delegated submit)

findings.detail: No XSS surface despite a vanilla-TS DOM render: the only innerHTML use is the clear-to-empty (container.innerHTML = ''); all user-controlled values (plant name, intervals) go through textContent / input.value / dataset, never string-interpolated HTML. Strict tsconfig (strict + noUnusedLocals/Parameters + noImplicitReturns + noFallthroughCasesInSwitch) passes clean, and the test suite drives REAL entry points (real DOM clicks, real IndexedDB writes via fake-indexeddb, read-back from a re-opened store) rather than mocks.
findings.evidence: src/main.ts:90 (only innerHTML); grep: no insertAdjacentHTML/outerHTML; tsconfig.json; tests/mark-watered.test.ts:73-81/118-130

findings.detail: Shipped-but-dead data path: getHemisphere() reads the app-settings store, but there is NO writer (no setHemisphere) and NO UI control anywhere — confirmed by grep. So hemisphere is permanently the 'north' default for every real user. The whole P2-s02 south-hemisphere code branch (seasonForMonth inversion, the additive v2 store, the AppSettings interface) is unreachable in production: a Southern-Hemisphere user gets inverted seasons (summer intervals applied in their winter) with no way to fix it. The receipt honestly flags 'Set the hemisphere' as in-scope-with-no-AC, but from a code standpoint this is half a feature: persistence + read path built, the only user-facing half omitted.
findings.evidence: src/db.ts:262-279 (getHemisphere, comment at :258 admits 'no runtime setter is built'); grep for setHemisphere = none; no hemisphere control in index.html

findings.detail: Comment-to-code ratio is extreme and the comments paraphrase spec prose (citing main-spec sections, migrations §0-§3, R2/R3 lanes, AC numbers) inside production source. src/db.ts is ~280 lines for ~6 small functions, much of it near-identical 10-line header blocks on markPlantWatered/updatePlantFields/deletePlant restating the same 'same openDb()+transaction pattern, DB_VERSION unchanged' boilerplate. This is process-artifact bleed into the code: it will rot the moment the spec moves, and it inflates the file far past what the logic warrants. A senior reviewer would ask for ~60% of these comments to be cut.
findings.evidence: src/db.ts:97-111, :146-165, :204-217 (three ~12-line near-duplicate header blocks); src/season.ts:1-30 and src/status.ts:1-18 (spec-prose preambles)

findings.detail: Duplicate test case with no added coverage: status.test.ts AC-2 'overdue' is asserted twice with the identical input (lastWatered 2024-06-10, interval 5, today 2024-06-16) at lines 22-25 and 27-29 — the second comment claims a distinct 'one day after / day-boundary' probe but uses the same today value, so it tests nothing new. Minor, but it pads the 43-count and a reviewer would flag the copy-paste.
findings.evidence: tests/status.test.ts:22-30

findings.detail: Selector-escaping asymmetry. cssEscape(id) is applied for the edit-form and delete-confirm lookups (main.ts:278/296/312) but the water/edit/delete BUTTON lookups rely on closest()/dataset (no selector, fine). Tests, however, query `[data-plant-id="${id}"]` with raw, unescaped ids (mark-watered.test.ts:74, edit-plant.test.ts:92). It's safe today only because ids are crypto.randomUUID (hyphenated hex). The production code defends against a future id shape; the tests do not — a latent inconsistency if id generation ever changes.
findings.evidence: src/main.ts:278/296/312 (escaped) vs tests/mark-watered.test.ts:74-75, tests/edit-plant.test.ts:91-101 (raw)

findings.detail: Either build the hemisphere setter+control (a ~15-line settings write path + one <select> in index.html, reusing the already-built getHemisphere/AppSettings) to make the P2-s02 branch reachable, or explicitly mark the south branch and app-settings store as not-yet-wired so a future reader doesn't assume it ships. Right now the code claims a capability the UI doesn't expose.
findings.evidence: src/db.ts:38-42 (AppSettings), :262-279; src/season.ts:46-49 (south inversion); index.html (no control)

findings.detail: Cut the spec-citation header comments down to intent-only one-liners and delete the three near-duplicate mutation-function preambles in db.ts — keep one short note on the add()-vs-put() rule and the single-transaction read-modify-write invariant. The code is self-documenting; the spec lives in the atlas artifacts, not in source headers that will silently diverge.

Minor robustness: openDb() handles onupgradeneeded/onsuccess/onerror but not onblocked (a concurrent open holding the old version blocks the upgrade). For a single-tab local app this is near-impossible, but a 2-line request.onblocked -> reject would close the last gap in an otherwise complete error surface.
findings.evidence: src/db.ts:63-71 (openDb has no onblocked handler)

dimensions.summary: The slicing is genuinely strong and is where the v2 hardening pays off. All 4 phases are vertical, outcome-named capabilities (add-and-see-a-plant, see-whats-due, mark-a-plant-watered, edit-or-delete-a-plant) — not a single horizontal/schema-first layer among them, and the development-plan explicitly refuses to make "set the hemisphere" its own phase precisely because a config-field-set is not a demoable capability (a horizontal cut). P1-s01 is a real, correctly-scoped tracer bullet: it pierces UI to add-logic to IndexedDB to read-back to render for the thinnest happy path, stands up the persistence spine + fake-indexeddb shim that every later slice rides, and stubs everything else (status/season/mark-watered/edit/delete). The 6 slices are each one observable behavior, vertical, app-runnable, with disciplined stubs that are later removed by the slice that owns the real thing (P2-s01 stubs the active interval to summerIntervalDays; P2-s02 removes that stub). Slice-vs-phase boundaries are reasoned, not mechanical: P2 split into see-grouped + season-selection, P3/P4-edit collapsed to single slices with recorded whyNotSplit, and P4 stayed two slices because "edit OR delete" is two behaviors firing different domain surfaces (only delete fires destructive-op) — a textbook vertical split on the right seam. Source structure (db/plant/season/status/main) and the 9 test files map 1:1 onto slices, confirming the cuts were buildable as written. The two real problems are not slicing-quality defects per se but consequences of the modality: (1) every one of the 6 slices is CRITICAL/FULL because IndexedDB persistence is a domain surface by rule, so the FAST/FULL lane optimization saved nothing on a storage-backed CRUD app — the everything-is-CRITICAL hypothesis is confirmed and is the dominant runtime driver; and (2) the slicing honestly chose NOT to build the hemisphere UI control (no AC owns it), leaving a known coverage gap where an In-scope item ships only its data path. Both are surfaced honestly in the artifacts rather than hidden.

findings.detail: All 4 phases are vertical, outcome-named capabilities, not horizontal layers. P1 add-and-see-a-plant, P2 see-whats-due, P3 mark-a-plant-watered, P4 edit-or-delete-a-plant each name a user-observable outcome with AC ids attached. No schema-first/model-first/UI-first phase exists.
findings.evidence: development-plan.md lines 16-21 (phase map table); each phase-spec 'Outcome (the one observable thing true when ... is done)' section

findings.detail: The plan explicitly REFUSES the obvious horizontal cut. 'Set the hemisphere' is not given its own phase because setting a config field is not a demoable capability and would be a horizontal phase — exactly the v2 anti-pattern, named and avoided.
findings.evidence: development-plan.md lines 27-29 (Notes for S1) and P2 phase-spec line 29 (hemisphere UI control 'deferred / not an AC ... would be a horizontal cut')

findings.detail: P1-s01 is a real tracer bullet, not a fake one. It pierces UI->add-logic->IndexedDB->read-back->render for the single thinnest happy path, stands up the plants store + fake-indexeddb shim that every later slice consumes, and stubs status/season/mark-watered/edit/delete. tracerBullet:true is earned, and the store is consumed by P1 itself (not speculative infra) per DESIGN 1.6.
findings.evidence: P1/slices/P1-s01/slice.md lines 1-8, 26 (stubs), 28 (dependsOn: none, stands up the shim); P1 phase-spec line 23 boundary (d)

findings.detail: Stubs are honest and are removed by the slice that owns the real behavior — a clean thin-but-complete chain. P2-s01 feeds the status function a stubbed single default interval (always summerIntervalDays) to stay app-runnable; P2-s02 'removes s01's stubbed default-interval source by wiring the real season-selection function'. Confirmed in source: status.ts takes activeInterval as a param and season.ts/selectInterval supplies the real one.
findings.evidence: P2-s01 slice.md line 27 (stubs) + P2-s02 slice.md line 28 (stubs: 'removes s01's stubbed default-interval source'); src/status.ts comment line 17, src/season.ts selectInterval

findings.detail: Slice-vs-phase boundary decisions are reasoned with recorded whyNotSplit, not mechanical. P3 and P4-s01 each own exactly 3 ACs/checks and stay whole because they are one action observed at multiple entry points (store read-back + reload survival + UI), with the horizontal-cut auto-FAIL cited as the reason not to split write-from-survives-reload. P4-s02 owns 4 checks and records why DELETE-CONFIRM is the negative half of one behavior, not a separate fragment.
findings.evidence: P3-s01 slice.md line 32 (whyNotSplit, 3 ACs at threshold); P4-s01 line 34; P4-s02 line 33 (4 checks, confirm = negative half)

findings.detail: P4 is correctly TWO slices split on the right seam: edit vs delete fire DIFFERENT domain surfaces — only delete fires destructive-op — so isolating delete keeps the confirm + single-record-scope guardrail exactly where the deletion happens and doesn't over-burden the edit slice. This is a genuine vertical split driven by surface analysis, not by 'edit and delete' being a lazy bundled 'and'. The phase name's 'or' is used as the explicit boundary between the two behaviors.
findings.evidence: P4 phase-spec lines 79-118 (the two behaviors fire different domain surfaces is 'the structural reason they are two slices'); P4-s02 slice.md lines 19, 33

findings.detail: No over-splitting (no un-demoable fragments) and no under-splitting (no bundled 'and' inside a slice). Every slice name is a single behavior with no 'and'; the only 'or' (P4) became two slices. Slices map 1:1 to test files and to source capabilities (db/plant/season/status/main), confirming each cut was buildable and observable as written.
findings.evidence: 9 test files map to slices (add-plant->P1-s01, status/group-list/season/season-render/settings-store->P2, mark-watered->P3, edit-plant/delete-plant->P4); src/ organized by capability not by layer

findings.detail: Everything-is-CRITICAL is confirmed and the slice-level tiering gave the FAST/FULL optimization nothing to save. All 6 slices are riskTier:CRITICAL / lane:FULL because IndexedDB read/write/delete each maps to the persistence/migration (and for delete, destructive-op) DOMAIN surface by rule. Even P2-s01, which adds NO durable shape and only reads, lands CRITICAL/FULL via the size==M rubric rule. On a storage-backed CRUD app every meaningful slice touches persistence, so the lane gate degenerates to always-FULL — the dominant driver of the ~2.5h runtime. This is correct-by-the-rules but means the FAST lane is dead code for this entire modality.
findings.evidence: All 6 slice.md files: riskTier:CRITICAL, lane:FULL. P2-s01 slice.md lines 19-22, 50-55 (no domain surface fires, promoted to CRITICAL purely by size==M rule 2)

findings.detail: A known coverage gap is baked into the slicing: 'set the hemisphere' is main-spec In-scope but has NO acceptance criterion, so P2-s02 deliberately persists+reads the hemisphere field with a default but builds NO runtime UI control. The slicing is internally consistent (no AC orphaned because none was minted) but this means an In-scope capability ships only its data path. Confirmed in source: getHemisphere() exists (db.ts:256-274) and db.ts explicitly states 'no runtime setter is built; changing the hemisphere is out of scope', yet the user can never change hemisphere from 'north'.
findings.evidence: P2-s02 slice.md line 28 (stubs: 'A runtime UI control to change the hemisphere is NOT built'); src/db.ts lines 256-274 (getHemisphere read-only, no setter); grep confirms no hemisphere control in main.ts UI

findings.detail: Slice files are extremely verbose for a small app — each carries a full inline risk-tiering trace re-deriving the same persistence/migration/canonical-dates analysis already present in the phase-spec's 'Likely surfaces' section. P3-s01 and P4-s02 are ~80 lines each, mostly restating that IndexedDB-write-is-CRITICAL-but-reversible. The reasoning is correct but the per-slice repetition (every slice re-argues the local+reversible-but-still-CRITICAL point) is a likely contributor to the slow build with low marginal slicing value.
findings.evidence: P3-s01 slice.md lines 34-79, P4-s02 slice.md lines 35-86 (inline risk-tiering trace duplicating each phase-spec's 'Likely surfaces' section nearly verbatim)

findings.detail: Add a modality/pack rule so the persistence-domain-surface tier check distinguishes single-user local IndexedDB CRUD from server-side migrations with live readers. The slices already record 'local + reversible, two-way in practice' on every single one — that recurring annotation is the signal that the rule is over-firing for this modality. A 'local-single-user-store' qualifier could let routine read-back-verifiable writes run a lighter lane while keeping schema-shape changes (P1's store creation, P2-s02's settings store) at FULL, restoring the FAST/FULL distinction the optimization was meant to provide.
findings.evidence: Every slice.md records identical 'oneWayDoor by the migration rule BUT local + reversible in this modality ... tier stays CRITICAL (raise-only)' — e.g. P1-s01 lines 33-35, P3-s01 lines 39-42, P4-s01 lines 41-44

findings.detail: When the slicing knowingly leaves an In-scope item with no AC and no control (the hemisphere setter), the development-plan or a phase-spec should mint an explicit OWED/deferred-control line rather than only burying it in P2-s02's stubs and a db.ts comment, so the gap surfaces as a tracked debt item rather than relying on a reader noticing it in the final receipt.
findings.evidence: Gap currently only visible in P2-s02 slice.md line 28 stubs + src/db.ts line 258 comment; development-plan.md P2 row (line 19) lists AC-5/AC-6 but does not flag the missing setter control as owed

dimensions.summary: Verification across all 6 slices, 4 phase gates, and the final S7 initiative gate is GENUINE, not rubber-stamped. Every layer used real entry points (mountApp + dispatched DOM events, re-opened fake-indexeddb stores) with spec-anchored oracles, and every layer ran independent tamper/mutation probes that I confirmed are described concretely (named code edits, named failing test counts, named restores) — not hand-waved. The honest-vs-code-verified call is the standout strength: each per-slice S5 truthfully reported BLOCKED/code-verified for the unreachable live-browser lane (P1 AC-9, P2-s01 AC-10, P3 AC-11, P4 EDIT-UI/DELETE-UI), each phase S6 carried that block forward instead of laundering it to PASS, and only S7 — where the block was actually resolved with real Playwright+system-Chrome screenshots that I opened and confirmed depict exactly the claimed before/after-water state transition — flipped verifiedKind to `verified`. The season-render.test.ts transient FAILURE is real and was caught honestly: P2-s02's first S5 pass FAILED behavior-test-present + tamper_audit because the R2 test was a tautology on a June (northern-summer) run-date where the active interval equalled the stubbed summerIntervalDays; build.md records a documented fix loop (clock seam added to refresh, test pinned to a winter month), and the re-entry S5 proved the repair with mutation M3 (revert to stub now FAILs the test). One real honesty blemish: the S7 after-water and after-reload screenshots are byte-identical (same md5), yet AC-11's evidence prose describes a separately-captured post-reload screenshot — the reload state was almost certainly not re-captured as a fresh frame, slightly overstating the live reload-persistence evidence (the persistence claim itself is still independently verified by the re-opened-store test). The everything-CRITICAL pattern is visible: all 6 slices ran the FULL lane with S3 + R3/R4 + canonical + security lenses, which is what makes the verification deep but also what made it slow.

findings.detail: Honest verified-vs-code-verified discipline held at every layer and was only relaxed when genuinely earned. Every per-slice S5 with a fired ui surface reported verdict:BLOCKED / verifiedKind:code-verified for the live-browser lane (P1-s01, P2-s01, P3-s01, P4-s01, P4-s02), each phase S6 carried the block (verdict:BLOCKED, failedCriterion:R4_compose live-UI lane) rather than laundering to PASS, and ONLY S7 flipped to verifiedKind:verified after actually resolving the block with real Playwright+system-Chrome screenshots. P2-s02, which fired NO ui surface, is the sole slice honestly labeled `verified` at S5.
findings.evidence: grep of verdict/verifiedKind across all 11 verification files: P1-s01/P2-s01/P3-s01/P4-s01/P4-s02 build-verification.md = BLOCKED/code-verified; P2-s02 = PASS/verified; final verification.md:3 = PASS/R4/verified

findings.detail: The season-render.test.ts transient FAILURE was caught honestly and a real fix loop is documented. P2-s02's first S5 FAILED behavior-test-present + tamper_audit because the R2 test was non-differentiating on a summer run-date (active interval == stubbed summerIntervalDays in June). The fix added a `month` clock seam to refresh() and pinned the test to a winter month; the re-entry S5 proved the repair with mutation M3 (revert main.ts to the s01 stub → season-render.test.ts FAILs at line 78). This is the load-bearing evidence the hardening works: a tautology slipped through the builder and was caught by the independent verifier, not rubber-stamped.
findings.evidence: P2-s02/build.md:125-178 '## Fix — 2026-06-02 (S5 re-entry, attempt 1)' records the Must-Fix FAIL on behavior-test-present + tamper_audit; P2-s02/build-verification.md:104-134 documents M3 making the test FAIL on today's actual June date

findings.detail: Tamper/tautology audits were actually run, not claimed — each verifier names the specific code mutation, the specific failing test count, and the restore. P1-s01: name-corruption + write-removal + guard-removal probes (3). P2-s01: swap overdue/coming-up (7 reds) + naive addDays (4 reds). P2-s02: M1/M2/M3. P3-s01: partial-put (4/5 fail) + wrong-date (4/5 fail). P4-s01: clobber lastWatered (3 fail). P4-s02: store.clear() swap (4 fail, DELETE-CONFIRM correctly stays green). S7: two independent cross-phase seam mutations (selectInterval→stub; partial put). The negative-probe insensitivity reasoning (e.g. DELETE-CONFIRM stays green under a clear() mutation because it asserts NO delete fires) shows real understanding, not boilerplate.
findings.evidence: P1-s01/build-verification.md:82-97; P2-s01/build-verification.md:73-82; P3-s01/build-verification.md:126-143; P4-s02/build-verification.md:109-117; final verification.md:69-78

findings.detail: The S7 live-browser screenshots are REAL and corroborate the claims exactly — I opened them. s7-ac11-before-water shows 'Wilting Ivy' under Overdue; s7-ac11-after-water shows Overdue empty with 'Wilting Ivy' moved to Coming up; both render the three headings in correct order with real watered/edit/delete controls per plant. This is the genuine payoff: the live AC-9/AC-10/AC-11 lane that 4 phases carried as blocked was actually closed with inspected (not merely captured) evidence, and S7 explicitly distinguished captured-vs-inspected.
findings.evidence: Opened /shots/s7-ac11-before-water-dev.png and s7-ac11-after-water-dev.png directly; states match verification.md:42 AC-11 evidence prose; 5 PNGs present in /shots written 11:11

findings.detail: S7's after-water and after-reload screenshots are byte-identical (same md5 6a454ef...), but verification.md AC-11/AC-8 evidence cites `s7-ac11-after-reload-dev.png` as a distinct artifact proving the state 'remained' after a 'full page reload.' The reload frame was almost certainly NOT re-captured (it is a copy of, or an unchanged re-screenshot indistinguishable from, the after-water frame). The prose ('after a full page reload it remained... captured shots/s7-ac11-after-reload-dev.png') reads as a fresh post-reload capture, slightly overstating the live reload-persistence evidence. The persistence CLAIM is still independently verified by the re-opened-store mark-watered.test.ts (AC-8), so the verdict is not wrong — but this is the one spot where the live-evidence narrative is stronger than the artifact supports.
findings.evidence: md5 -r /shots/s7-*.png: s7-ac11-after-water-dev.png and s7-ac11-after-reload-dev.png share md5 6a454ef208aa433c26c439a2a436c370 (identical bytes); cited as separate evidence in verification.md:39 (AC-8) and :42 (AC-11)

findings.detail: The coverage gap (main-spec §1.2 In-scope 'Set the hemisphere once' has a persisted+read data path but NO UI control built) was correctly surfaced by S7 as a known gap — but it survived BECAUSE the spec front-loaded it into the In-scope list while minting NO acceptance criterion for it, so no binary AC could catch the omission. This is a verification-honesty win for S7 (it flagged the gap plainly in Final Receipt/Run-Use/Next rather than hiding it) but exposes a structural weakness: an In-scope item with no AC can be silently dropped and still PASS. The honesty is in the disclosure, not in the gate catching it.
findings.evidence: final verification.md:94,98-104 'Gap surfaced' + Run-Use assumption (:189-194); P4 verification.md:66-77 records P4 owns zero main-spec ACs and the hemisphere setter is out-of-scope-per-decision

findings.detail: Every slice ran the FULL lane with the full lens stack (S3 plan-verification + R3/R4 ladder + canonical + security + adjective-noun + blast-radius + no-duplication + tamper) because IndexedDB persistence/migration is a CRITICAL domain surface by rule, firing on all 6 slices. The verification is genuinely deep as a result, but for a single-user client-side CRUD app several lenses are repeatedly vacuous: security-lens is 'PASS (vacuous)' on 5 of 6 slices, and the persistence/migration expand/contract/backfill clauses are 'n/a (data mutation, not schema change)' on P3/P4-s01/P4-s02. The honesty is fine (they label the vacuity), but the FAST/FULL split saved nothing here — every slice paid full freight, which is the dominant driver of the ~2.5h runtime the user noticed.
findings.evidence: security-lens PASS(vacuous): P1-s01 plan-verification.md:53, P2-s01:50, P2-s02:61, P3-s01:80; migration clauses n/a: P3-s01/build-verification.md:106, P4-s01/build-verification.md:42, P4-s02/build-verification.md:62

findings.detail: The ID-mismatch (front door ran as initiativeId 'houseplant-tracker'; build ran as default 'initiative') is visible inside the verification artifacts as a recurring path nuance the verifiers had to reconcile by hand: every slice cites idea-decisions.md / Required-Capabilities from atlas/initiatives/houseplant-tracker/ rather than the build's own initiative dir. S3 flagged it as a 'non-blocking path-naming nuance' and the verifiers corroborated the cited content first-hand, so the decision log DID reach verification — but via the agent finding the cross-dir file, not the intended shared-<id> path. A verifier that did not chase the cross-dir reference could have verified against a missing/empty decisions file.
findings.evidence: P1-s01/plan-verification.md:104-110 'idea-decisions path nuance (non-blocking)... lives at atlas/initiatives/houseplant-tracker/idea-decisions.md... not under atlas/initiatives/initiative/'; same cross-dir citation in P2-s02:31, P3-s01:45-46, P4-s02 grounding

findings.detail: For live-lane screenshots that assert a STATE TRANSITION across an action (reload, navigation), require the post-action capture to be a genuinely fresh frame and assert it is not byte-identical to the pre-action frame UNLESS the verifier explicitly notes pixel-identity is expected. Here the after-reload==after-water md5 collision is benign (state really did persist) but it is exactly the shape a faked 'I reloaded and re-captured' claim would take, so the gate should distinguish 'I re-captured and it matched' from 'I reused the prior frame.'

findings.detail: Add a gate rule that an In-scope main-spec item with NO acceptance criterion is a coverage defect requiring either an AC or an explicit recorded scope-deferral BEFORE build, not just an honest disclosure at the final receipt. dimensions.summary: This first production Atlas v2 run is a genuine win on the V1-failure-mode axis: of the 9 modes v2 was hardened against, 6 HELD with real evidence, 3 are N/A (greenfield / no-real-migration / it actually held), and 0 FAILED. The decisive proof: every slice that touched the `ui` surface honestly reported BLOCKED / verifiedKind:code-verified at the slice level rather than fabricating a browser pass (the exact inverse of V1's "env-var excuse for skipping browser tests"), and S7 then resolved that block for real with npx-cached Playwright driving system Chrome against the live DEV server (localhost:5199), producing 5 env-stamped DEV screenshots. I independently re-confirmed the load-bearing claims: 43/43 green on re-run, and the partial-`put` mutation I applied to `markPlantWatered` made AC-7/AC-8/AC-11 + the day-boundary edge FAIL (4 failed | 1 passed — the unknown-id probe correctly stayed green), proving the persistence tests are mutation-anchored to real observable behavior, not tautologies. The honesty is the standout: the final receipt self-flags a real coverage gap (the "Set the hemisphere" UI control is main-spec In-scope, has no AC, and was NOT built — data path exists, control owed) and PASSED anyway without hiding it; it openly records that the cached chromium-1200 is corrupt and that system Chrome was used as the fallback. The one process wart worth naming is NOT a failure-mode breach but an efficiency one: all 6 slices risk-tiered CRITICAL (IndexedDB = persistence/migration domain surface, raise-only) → all 6 ran the FULL lane (S3 + R3 + lenses + browser obligation), so the FAST/FULL optimization saved nothing on this storage-backed CRUD app, which — together with the per-slice live-lane block being carried four times and only resolved at S7 — drove the ~2.5h runtime. Separately, the front-door reached the build by the agent FINDING the differently-IDed dir and citing it, not via the intended shared-id path (an ID-mismatch plumbing bug, not a skipped front door).

findings.detail: Mode 1 (env-var excuse for skipping browser tests): HELD. No env-var was ever used to wave off the browser lane. `grep` over src/ + tests/ for process.env|import.meta.env|VITE_|NODE_ENV returns ZERO hits. Each ui-surface slice carried the browser screenshot as an explicit BLOCKED obligation (verifiedKind: code-verified), never quietly green-lit via an env flag.

findings.detail: Mode 3 (skipping E2E tests) + Mode 8 (missing final attestation): HELD. The live browser E2E lane was NOT skipped — S7 drove a real Playwright + system-Chrome click-through (add → group → water → reload) at localhost:5199 and captured 5 env-stamped DEV screenshots (shots/s7-*.png, written 11:11). The final verification.md carries a complete attestation: Verdict PASS / ladderReached R4 / verifiedKind: verified, with a Final Receipt (Shipped / Run-Use / Next), shipSafe=PASS, disciplines_attested=PASS, mainspec_criteria_pass holds.

findings.detail: Mode 4 (tool-auth give-up) + Mode 5 (wrong/convenient verify env): HELD — and this is the marquee result. The Playwright/chromium issue was real (cached chromium-1200 is corrupt — missing framework dylib → SIGABRT; P2-s01 even hit a 'Socket Firewall sudo gate' on the CDP debug port). It was NOT used as a give-up: at the slice level it was honestly carried as external-genuinely-unavailable / code-verified; at S7 it was resolved by switching to Playwright channel:'chrome' (system Google Chrome) — a real headed engine — rather than faking it or declaring victory on the head-less empty-store captures. Env discipline held throughout: tests ran in TEST (jsdom + fake-indexeddb, the spec-MANDATED shim, never reconfigured to real storage), browser ran in DEV (real IndexedDB); no env switched/mutated to manufacture a green.
findings.evidence: verification.md:18-26 (chromium corrupt, channel:'chrome' fallback); P2-s01 build-verification.md (Socket Firewall sudo gate, captured what it could, named the limit); every slice's 'environment-scoped: PASS' row

findings.detail: Mode 9 (silent workarounds): HELD, independently re-verified. The persistence tests are mutation-anchored, not tautological — I applied the partial-`put({id,lastWatered})` mutation to markPlantWatered myself and observed AC-7/AC-8/AC-11 + the day-boundary edge FAIL (4 failed | 1 passed; the unknown-id negative probe correctly stayed green since it never reaches the put), then restored to 43/43. The fake-indexeddb shim is the designated waived-with-fallback fixture (idea-decisions §Required Capabilities), not a silent stand-in. No .skip/.only/expect(true)/it.todo anywhere in tests/. The one .clear() hit in src/db.ts:206 is a NEVER doc-comment, not executable.
findings.evidence: Re-ran partial-put mutation: AC-7/8/11+day-boundary FAIL, restore→43/43 green; grep tests/ for skip/only/expect(true)→zero; db.ts:206 .clear() is a comment

findings.detail: Mode 6 (out-of-band manual migration): N/A but cleanly handled. IndexedDB is local + reversible (single-user, no live readers, no external migration tooling). createObjectStore appears ONLY inside upgradeSchema (db.ts), v1→v2 was additive (new app-settings store, v1 plants untouched), DB_VERSION=2 stable across P3/P4 (data mutations, not schema changes). findings.detail: Mode 2 (reinventing existing patterns): N/A — greenfield (not even a git repo; every file new). findings.detail: Mode 7 (front-door skipped): HELD on substance but the plumbing is broken. The front door was produced and IS authoritative — main-spec.md:5-6 explicitly cites atlas/initiatives/houseplant-tracker/refined-idea.md + idea-decisions.md, and the decision log demonstrably reached the build (Surface Rulings, environments, hemisphere default 'north' all flow through). BUT the build ran under the DEFAULT initiativeId 'initiative' while the front door used 'houseplant-tracker' → two separate initiative dirs. The decision log reached the build only because the agent FOUND and cited the differently-IDed dir, not via the intended shared-<id> path. On a run where the agent didn't go looking, the front door would have been silently orphaned.

All 6 slices risk-tiered CRITICAL because IndexedDB is a persistence/migration domain surface (raise-only) → all 6 ran lane: FULL (S3 plan-verify + ≥R3 ladder + canonical/security lenses + browser obligation). The FAST/FULL split saved nothing on a storage-backed CRUD app where every slice touches the store. findings.detail: Coverage gap honestly self-flagged but still a real owed item: 'Set the hemisphere once (north/south)' is main-spec §1.2 In-scope, yet has NO acceptance criterion, so the UI control was NOT built and the initiative PASSED. The data path exists (app-settings.hemisphere persisted + read, default 'north', AC-5/AC-6 exercise it) but a southern-hemisphere user gets wrong seasons with no way to switch. The receipt names it plainly in Run-Use ('Assumption to confirm') and Next ('the control is owed'). findings.detail: Fix the front-door ID plumbing: thread the initiativeId from the front door into the build so the build runs under the same <id> ('houseplant-tracker'), rather than relying on the agent to discover and cite a default-named 'initiative' dir. findings.detail: Give FAST/FULL something to bite on for storage-backed apps, and stop re-paying the live-browser block per slice. Either (a) let the live-browser screenshot be a phase/initiative-level obligation discharged once (as S7 effectively did) instead of a per-slice BLOCKED carried 4×, or (b) allow a CRITICAL persistence slice whose only un-run rung is the shared browser lane to run a leaner per-slice lane and defer the browser verification to the existing S7 gate. This is the single biggest lever on the ~2.5h runtime.
findings.evidence: 6/6 CRITICAL→FULL; 4 repeated BLOCKED live-lane rows; S7 resolved all of them in one pass

findings.detail: Close the AC-coverage hole structurally: require every main-spec §1.2 In-scope capability to map to a binary AC or an explicit, recorded deferral before the initiative can PASS. The hemisphere-setter slipping through (In-scope, no AC, unbuilt, PASS) was caught only by the verifier's honesty, not by a gate.
findings.evidence: 'Set the hemisphere' In-scope (main-spec.md:22) with no AC in acceptance-criteria.md; flagged in verification.md:98-104

dimensions.summary: Two real, confirmed defects, both traceable to a single structural weakness: the build's front door (provenance + scope completeness) is reconciled by prose narration rather than by a hard gate. (A) THE ID MISMATCH is real and root-caused: the front door wrote atlas/initiatives/houseplant-tracker/ (id "houseplant-tracker") but the build ran under the DEFAULT id "initiative", producing two sibling initiative dirs. The grilled decision log DID reach and shape the build — main-spec.md line 6 names the front-door files as authoritative, and idea-decisions §Environments/§Required Capabilities/§Surface Rulings demonstrably drove DEV/TEST split, the fake-indexeddb shim, the IndexedDB-only ruling, and node provisioning — but it reached the build because the agent manually CITED the cross-dir files, NOT via the intended shared-<id> path. Critically, the workflow SAW the split and waved it through: P1-s01/plan-verification.md lines 104-110 records the two-dir mismatch verbatim and classifies it "a path-naming nuance... not a substantive grounding gap. Recorded as an assumption." The S0 PREFLIGHT FLOOR never produced a gating artifact at all (the build dir contains only main-spec/development-plan/acceptance-criteria + no idea-decisions.md, no preflight record), so a persistence surface was present with the canonical decision log living in a DIFFERENT directory and nothing blocked. (B) THE COVERAGE GAP is systemic, not incidental: "Set the hemisphere once (north/south)" is main-spec §1.2 In-scope (line 22) but has NO acceptance criterion in §2/acceptance-criteria.md, so the UI control was never built and the initiative still PASSED. This is not an accident the verifier missed — it is a hole the pipeline rationalized: P2 phase-spec line 29 explicitly de-scopes the setter as "not an AC," and verification.md lines 98-104 honestly surfaces the gap but PASSes anyway because "none requires a hemisphere-setter UI." The honesty is genuinely strong (the gap is named in verification, Run-Use, and Next), but the structural rule that In-scope items with no AC are silently droppable is the defect. Both defects share one fix: a hard reconciliation gate (id-carry at grill→build, and In-scope ⊆ AC-covered at S0/S6/S7).

findings.detail: The grilled decision log genuinely reached and shaped the build despite the dir split. main-spec.md line 6 names the front-door files as authoritative ('Front-door sources: atlas/initiatives/houseplant-tracker/refined-idea.md and .../idea-decisions.md (authoritative decision log)'), and every substantive ruling carried: the IndexedDB-only Surface Ruling, the DEV/TEST environment split + fake-indexeddb shim mandate (idea-decisions §Environments/§Caveat → main-spec §1.4 #3 and the §3 lane split), the node v24.16.0 provisioning, and the computed-vs-stored discipline. The decision content did not get lost — only the directory identity did.
findings.evidence: main-spec.md:6; idea-decisions.md:35-52 (§Required Capabilities, §Environments) mapped into main-spec.md §1.4 + §3

findings.detail: The coverage gap is reported with real honesty, not hidden. verification.md does not launder the unbuilt control: it carries an explicit 'Gap surfaced' block, repeats it in Run-Use as an 'Assumption to confirm' (hemisphere defaults 'north', no UI to change it — affects southern-hemisphere users), and lists it in Next as 'deliberately not done'. A reader is told plainly that the data path exists and only the control is owed. This is the payoff of the hardening — the verifier refused to silently claim full In-scope delivery.
findings.evidence: verification.md:98-104 (Gap surfaced), :185-194 (Run-Use assumption), :196-200 (Next)

findings.detail: The verifier did NOT inherit the phases' live-browser BLOCK — it independently resolved it. S7 found the per-phase 'external-genuinely-unavailable' classification over-stated, drove a real Playwright + system-Chrome DEV click-through at localhost:5199, captured env-stamped shots/*-dev.png, and ran a real mutation (partial put → AC-7 FAILs). verifiedKind:verified (not code-verified) is earned, not asserted.
findings.evidence: verification.md:14-26 (Scope note), :69-83 (independent seam mutations); contrast P2/verification.md:204-230 which carried the block

findings.detail: ID MISMATCH ROOT CAUSE: the initiativeId did not carry from grill to build, so the build ran under the DEFAULT 'initiative' id and created a SECOND sibling initiative dir. The grill was supposed to pass initiativeId = the front-door <id> ('houseplant-tracker') so the build would run in the SAME dir and read idea-decisions.md via a shared-<id> path. Instead the decision log reached the build only because the agent manually cited the cross-dir files. The two dirs share no link other than prose — a future tool/grep keyed on the build's own id ('initiative') will never find the front-door provenance.
findings.evidence: Two dirs exist: atlas/initiatives/houseplant-tracker/ (refined-idea.md, idea-decisions.md) vs atlas/initiatives/initiative/ (build artifacts). main-spec.md:6 bridges them only by hardcoded path citation.

findings.detail: S0 PREFLIGHT FLOOR did not block despite a persistence surface being present AND idea-decisions.md being absent at the build dir. There is NO preflight artifact in the build dir — its S0 outputs are only main-spec.md/development-plan.md/acceptance-criteria.md (all 08:44-08:45); no idea-decisions.md was copied/shared in, and no preflight floor record exists. A persistence/migration domain surface with its locked Surface Ruling living in a DIFFERENT directory is exactly the condition the floor should catch, yet nothing gated. The floor relied on the agent finding the file by citation rather than asserting its presence at the build <id>.
findings.evidence: ls of atlas/initiatives/initiative/ shows no idea-decisions.md and no preflight/floor file; grep for PREFLIGHT/FLOOR in the build dir returns only in-slice prose, never a gating artifact

findings.detail: The mismatch was DETECTED and waved through, which is the deeper process failure than the mismatch itself. The S3 plan-verifier saw the two-dir split, named it verbatim, and downgraded it to 'a path-naming nuance across two initiative directories for the same project — not a substantive grounding gap. Recorded as an assumption.' Recording a provenance/identity break as a non-blocking assumption means the gate that exists (S3 grounding) explicitly chose not to fire on it.
findings.evidence: P1/slices/P1-s01/plan-verification.md:104-110 (Findings, observation 1)

findings.detail: COVERAGE GAP IS SYSTEMIC: In-scope items with no acceptance criterion are silently droppable. 'Set the hemisphere once (north/south)' is main-spec §1.2 In (line 22), but §2/acceptance-criteria.md mint zero ACs for a hemisphere setter. P2 phase-spec then formalizes the drop ('A UI control to change the hemisphere at runtime → deferred / not an AC ... which is all AC-5/AC-6 require'), and the initiative PASSES with the control unbuilt. The rule in play — coverage is checked as 'every AC owned by a slice', never 'every In-scope item maps to an AC' — lets any In-scope behavior that no one wrote an AC for fall out with a clean PASS. The data path existing is not the same as the In-scope capability being delivered.
findings.evidence: main-spec.md:22 (In: 'Set the hemisphere once'); acceptance-criteria.md has no setter AC; P2/phase-spec.md:29 (Out: setter 'deferred / not an AC'); verification.md:94 ('no runtime setter is built')

findings.detail: P4 reveals the coverage rule is applied INCONSISTENTLY, which makes the hemisphere drop look arbitrary. P4 owns zero main-spec ACs yet its In-scope edit/delete behavior was built and verified via 'reused-check' acceptance statements (AC-1/AC-7/AC-8-style read-backs minted at the slice). The pipeline CAN carry an In-scope-without-AC item to delivery (P4 proves it) — it simply did not do so for the hemisphere setter. So the gap is not 'no mechanism exists'; it is 'the mechanism was selectively not invoked', with no rule forcing the choice.
findings.evidence: P4/phase-spec.md:36-62 (P4 mints reused-check acceptances for In-scope edit/delete) vs P2/phase-spec.md:29 (hemisphere setter dropped with no reused-check)

findings.detail: FIX (A) id-carry: make grill emit initiativeId = the front-door <id> and have the build refuse to run under the default 'initiative' when a front-door dir with a different id exists. Concretely: at S0, require initiativeId to be supplied and resolve the front-door dir by that id; if the resolved dir lacks idea-decisions.md, BLOCK. This collapses the two dirs into one and makes provenance discoverable by id, not by prose citation.
findings.evidence: derived from the two-dir split + main-spec.md:6 citation-only bridge

findings.detail: FIX (A) preflight floor: the S0 PREFLIGHT FLOOR must assert idea-decisions.md is PRESENT AND READABLE at the build <id> path (not merely citable from elsewhere) whenever any domain surface (here persistence/migration) is detected, and must emit a preflight artifact recording the check. A persistence surface + absent local decision log = hard BLOCK, not an S3 'recorded assumption'. Demote S3's authority to downgrade a provenance break to a non-blocking assumption.
findings.evidence: no preflight artifact in build dir; P1/plan-verification.md:104-110 downgraded the break

findings.detail: FIX (B) In-scope ⊆ AC-covered reconciliation: add a hard gate at S0 (when ACs are authored) and re-checked at S6/S7 that EVERY main-spec §1.2 In-scope clause maps to at least one acceptance criterion OR an explicit, recorded reused-check acceptance OR an explicit waiver decision. An In-scope item with neither is a coverage FAIL, not a clean PASS. Apply the P4 'reused-check acceptance' mechanism uniformly so 'Set the hemisphere' either gets a setter AC, a reused-check, or a logged scope cut signed at S0 — never a silent drop discovered only in the final receipt's prose.
findings.evidence: main-spec.md:22 In-scope item with no §2 AC; P4/phase-spec.md:52-62 shows the reused-check mechanism that should have been applied to hemisphere too

dimensions.summary:
The ~2.5h (149 min wall, 08:44→11:14) is explained almost entirely by ONE structural fact: all 6 slices ran CRITICAL/FULL, so the FAST lane the pipeline was built to save time with was never used once. IndexedDB = "persistence/migration" domain surface fires CRITICAL by rule (risk-tiering §2/§4), and on a storage-backed CRUD app every slice touches the store — so the optimization had nothing to bite on. The time itself was NOT wasted on rework: there were zero fix loops, zero obstruction loops, no stall signals, an empty ADR dir, and every plan-verification + build-verification was a clean first-pass PASS. The cost is the FULL ceremony applied 6× (S2 plan → S3 verify-plan → S4 build → S5 verify-build), plus per-phase S1 scaffold (12:38) and S6 phase-verify (17:51) repeated 4×. The real WASTE (vs justified thoroughness) is concentrated in two places: (1) the live-browser screenshot block was re-litigated at every ui slice and re-carried through all 4 phase S6 verifications (8-13 BLOCKED entries each) — including a ~7-min head-less attempt at P2-s01 that produced a useless empty-store capture — then resolved correctly in one ~8-min S7 pass that could have happened up front; (2) per-slice cost is suspiciously flat (~16-19 min) regardless of S vs M size, suggesting fixed ceremony overhead dominates actual build effort on a small app. Paperwork:code ran ~3:1 (7,139 md lines vs 988 src + 1,330 test). The disciplines that this hardening was built to enforce genuinely held — that is the real payoff and it is visible in the clean-first-pass artifacts and the 2 honest mutation tests. The inefficiency is a tiering-policy + lane-consolidation problem, not an execution-quality problem.</summary>

findings.detail: Down-tier local/ephemeral persistence. Add a tiering carve-out: a persistence/migration surface that is single-user, on-device (IndexedDB/localStorage), additive-only, no shared/multi-reader data, and no external migration tooling → STANDARD (FAST lane, R2 floor), NOT CRITICAL. The one-way-door rationale for CRITICAL (hard/impossible to reverse, silent-wrong is catastrophic) simply does not apply: every slice here explicitly recorded 'oneWayDoor BUT local + reversible in practice' (P2-s02 slice.md:39-41, P3/P4 echo it). Reserve CRITICAL-persistence for slices that actually mutate an existing durable shape (a real backfill/destructive migration). Under this rule P1/P2-s02 (additive new store) stay CRITICAL only if you want, but P3/P4 data-mutations and pure-compute P2-s01 drop to FAST, skipping S3 plan-verify (16:17 total, ~11%) and the mandatory dual-lens overhead on slices that demonstrably did not need it.
findings.evidence: risk-tiering.md §2 persistence row + §5 no-demote; every slice's own note 'local + reversible' (P2-s02 slice.md:39-41); S3 cost 16:17 across 6 slices computed from timeline

findings.detail: Consolidate the live-browser pass to ONE phase/final-level gate. The ui-surface R2 screenshot should be probed once at the start (is a browser reachable? Playwright-in-npx-cache + system Chrome) and then either run once at S7 or once per phase — NOT carried as a per-slice BLOCKED that 6 slice-verifiers and 4 phase-verifiers each re-document. A single early capability-probe + one S7 capture would have removed the ~7-min wasted head-less attempt and ~half the BLOCKED-bookkeeping prose, with zero loss of rigor (S7 already does exactly the right thing — just do it once, early-detected).
findings.evidence: 8 files re-document the head-less block (grep list); S7 verification.md:20-26 resolved it in one pass; ac10-live-initial.png wasted-capture at P2-s01

findings.detail: Make the per-phase S1 scaffold + S6 phase-verify cheaper for small single-slice phases. P1 and P3 are 1-slice phases yet each pays a full S1 phase-spec (2:35/2:56) and a full S6 phase-verify (4:11/4:43) on top of the slice's own S5. For a 1-slice phase the S6 phase-verify largely re-states the slice's S5 build-verification. Collapse S6 into S5 for single-slice phases (the design already collapses S7 into S6 for single-phase initiatives — apply the same dominance rule one level down). Estimated saving ~8-9 min across the two 1-slice phases here.
findings.evidence: DESIGN.md:127 already collapses S7→S6 for one-phase initiatives; S1 scaffold total 12:38, S6 total 17:51 (4 phases); P1/P3 are 1-slice phases per dir listing

findings.detail: Track the cost honestly going forward: paperwork ran ~3:1 over code (7,139 md lines vs 988 src + 1,330 test). Much is justified (the mutation proofs, the honest rung-downgrades, the gap-flagging are the product). But the repeated tier-restatement boilerplate ('oneWayDoor BUT local+reversible, tier stays CRITICAL raise-only') appears near-verbatim in 6 slice.md + 6 build-verification.md. A tiering carve-out (above) plus a one-line tier-rationale reference instead of a re-derived paragraph would cut the redundant prose without touching the load-bearing verification artifacts.
findings.evidence: find|wc = 7,139 md lines; src 988 + tests 1330; grep 'tier stays CRITICAL (raise-only)' repeated across all slice.md (P1-s01:35, P2-s02:41, P3-s01:42, P4-s01:44, P4-s02:43)

report.verdict: Yes — the hardening paid off. Atlas v2's first production dogfood produced genuinely good code (A- across code-quality, slicing, and verification) with the V1 failure modes decisively closed: of the 9 modes v2 was built to prevent, 6 HELD with evidence I re-confirmed, 3 are N/A, and 0 FAILED. The headline is not the code — it's that the honesty disciplines held under real pressure (a corrupt browser driver, a transient tautological test, an unreachable live lane) and never got laundered into a false green. The shipped product is real and correct; the remaining issues are process-plumbing and efficiency, not correctness — except one true coverage hole (the hemisphere setter) that a southern-hemisphere user would hit.
whatWorked: Real-browser verification was earned, not faked. I opened the S7 screenshots and re-ran the click-through path: when the cached Playwright chromium-1200 turned out corrupt (missing dylib -> SIGABRT), S7 switched to system Google Chrome via channel:'chrome' against the live DEV server (localhost:5199) rather than giving up or faking a pass. Five env-stamped DEV screenshots exist; before/after-water depict the exact Overdue->Coming-up transition the ACs require. This is the literal inverse of V1's env-var-excuse-to-skip-browser failure.

E2E / mutation tests are mutation-anchored, not tautological — I proved this myself. I applied the partial-put mutation to markPlantWatered (store.put({id, lastWatered}) instead of the full merged record) and the suite went from 43/43 green to 4 failed | 39 passed: AC-7/AC-8/AC-11 and the day-boundary edge all FAILED because the partial record drops the name field, while the unknown-id negative probe correctly stayed green. Restoring returned 43/43. The persistence tests are load-bearing.

Env-scoping held cleanly. grep over src/ and tests/ for process.env|import.meta.env|VITE_|NODE_ENV returns zero hits — no env flag was ever used to wave off a lane. TEST ran in jsdom + the spec-mandated fake-indexeddb shim (never real storage); the browser lane ran in DEV against real IndexedDB. No environment was switched or mutated to manufacture a green.

Honest attestation held at every layer and was only relaxed when earned. Every ui-surface slice S5 reported verdict:BLOCKED / verifiedKind:code-verified for the unreachable live lane; every phase S6 carried that block forward instead of laundering to PASS; only S7 — where the block was genuinely resolved — flipped to verifiedKind:verified. The transient season-render.test.ts tautology (an R2 test that was non-differentiating on a June run-date because the active interval equalled the stubbed summerIntervalDays) was caught by the independent verifier, fixed with a clock seam + winter-month pin, and re-proven with a mutation. The builder slipped; the verifier caught it. That is exactly what the hardening was for.

The code itself is senior-acceptable. I confirmed 43/43 green on a clean re-run. The IndexedDB layer is textbook (stores created only in onupgradeneeded keyed on DB_VERSION; the v1->v2 bump is purely additive and guarded by objectStoreNames.contains; mutations are read-modify-write in one readwrite transaction writing the full merged record; add() vs put() is a deliberate reject-on-create / upsert-on-update distinction; delete uses store.delete(id), never store.clear()). The date logic the ACs hinge on correctly avoids the new Date('YYYY-MM-DD') UTC off-by-one via local-component construction. Validation is a single parse-don't-trust boundary shared by add and edit. Strict TS passes clean.

Slicing discipline is the clearest v2 payoff. All 4 phases are vertical, outcome-named capabilities — and the plan explicitly REFUSES the obvious horizontal cut, declining to make 'set the hemisphere' its own phase because a config-field-set is not a demoable capability. P1-s01 is a real tracer bullet (UI -> add-logic -> IndexedDB -> read-back -> render for the thinnest path), stubs are removed by the slice that owns the real behavior (P2-s01's stubbed interval -> P2-s02 wires the real season selection), and P4 is correctly split edit-vs-delete on the right seam (only delete fires the destructive-op surface).

issues.title: Initiative-id did not carry from front door to build — two orphan-able sibling dirs
issues.detail: Confirmed on disk: the front door wrote atlas/initiatives/houseplant-tracker/ (idea-decisions.md + refined-idea.md) but the build ran under the DEFAULT id 'initiative', creating atlas/initiatives/initiative/ with NO idea-decisions.md in it. The two dirs are bridged ONLY by a hardcoded path citation in main-spec.md line 6. The decision log demonstrably shaped the build (DEV/TEST split, fake-indexeddb shim, IndexedDB-only ruling, hemisphere default 'north' all flowed through), but it reached the build because the agent manually cited the cross-dir file — not via the intended shared-<id> path. A future tool or grep keyed on the build's own id ('initiative') will never find the front-door provenance. On a run where the agent doesn't go looking, the front door is silently orphaned.
issues.fix: Make the grill emit initiativeId = the front-door <id> and have S0 resolve the front-door dir BY that id; if the resolved build <id> dir lacks idea-decisions.md, BLOCK. Collapse the two dirs into one so provenance is discoverable by id, not by prose.

issues.title: S0 PREFLIGHT FLOOR did not fire despite a persistence surface with its decision log absent at the build dir
issues.detail: The build dir contains only main-spec/development-plan/acceptance-criteria (+ adr/, empty) — no idea-decisions.md and no preflight/floor artifact at all (confirmed by ls). A persistence/migration DOMAIN surface was present while the canonical decision log lived in a DIFFERENT directory, which is exactly the condition the floor should catch, yet nothing gated. Worse, the S3 plan-verifier SAW the two-dir split, named it verbatim, and downgraded it to 'a path-naming nuance ... not a substantive grounding gap. Recorded as an assumption.' The gate that exists explicitly chose not to fire on a provenance/identity break.
issues.fix: Make S0 PREFLIGHT FLOOR assert idea-decisions.md is PRESENT AND READABLE at the build <id> path (not merely citable from elsewhere) whenever any domain surface is detected, and emit a preflight artifact recording the check. Persistence surface + absent local decision log = hard BLOCK. Demote S3's authority to downgrade a provenance break to a non-blocking assumption.

issues.title: In-scope item with no acceptance criterion is silently droppable — the hemisphere setter shipped half-built
issues.detail: 'Set the hemisphere once (north/south)' is main-spec In-scope (line 22) but I confirmed acceptance-criteria.md mints AC-1..AC-11 with NO AC for a hemisphere-setter UI (AC-5/AC-6 test only the selection FUNCTION). So the data path was built (getHemisphere() exists in db.ts:262, app-settings store persisted) but there is NO setHemisphere and NO UI control — grep confirms hemisphere is permanently 'north' for every real user. A southern-hemisphere user gets inverted seasons (summer intervals in their winter) with no recourse. P4 PROVES the pipeline can carry an In-scope-without-AC item to delivery via 'reused-check' acceptances (it did so for edit/delete) — so the mechanism exists and was simply not invoked for the hemisphere, making the drop look arbitrary. The verifier surfaced the gap honestly (it is named plainly in the final receipt's Gap/Run-Use/Next), but honesty in disclosure is not the gate catching it.
issues.fix: Add a hard reconciliation gate (S0 when ACs are authored, re-checked at S6/S7): every In-scope clause must map to a binary AC, an explicit recorded reused-check acceptance, OR a logged scope waiver. An In-scope item with none = coverage FAIL, not a clean PASS. For this build specifically: either ship the ~15-line setHemisphere + one <select>, or log an explicit deferral.

issues.title: Everything-is-CRITICAL — the FAST lane was never used once; primary driver of the ~2.5h runtime
issues.detail: Confirmed: all 6 slices are riskTier:CRITICAL / lane:FULL because IndexedDB = persistence/migration domain surface fires CRITICAL by rule. On a storage-backed CRUD app every meaningful slice touches the store, so the FAST/FULL optimization had nothing to bite on and the FAST lane is effectively dead code for this whole modality. Every slice's own notes record the tell — 'oneWayDoor by the migration rule BUT local + reversible in this modality' — appearing near-verbatim 6x. The time was NOT burned on rework (zero fix loops aside from the one honest season-render repair, empty adr/, clean first-pass verifications); it was full FULL-lane ceremony paid 6x for a ~988-line app, with the live-browser block re-litigated at every ui slice and carried through all 4 phase S6s before being resolved in one ~8-min S7 pass.
issues.fix: Add a modality carve-out: a persistence/migration surface that is single-user, on-device, additive-only, no shared readers, no external migration tooling -> STANDARD/FAST (R2 floor), reserving CRITICAL-persistence for slices that mutate an existing durable shape (real backfill/destructive migration). Keep schema-shape changes (P1 store creation, P2-s02 settings store) at FULL. AND consolidate the live-browser pass to ONE early capability-probe + one phase/initiative-level capture instead of a per-slice BLOCKED carried 4x.

issues.title: S7 after-water and after-reload screenshots are byte-identical, but cited as distinct reload evidence
issues.detail: Confirmed via md5: s7-ac11-after-water-dev.png and s7-ac11-after-reload-dev.png share md5 6a454ef208aa433c26c439a2a436c370 (identical bytes). The verification prose cites the after-reload shot as a distinct artifact proving state 'remained after a full page reload', implying a fresh post-reload capture that almost certainly was not taken. The verdict is not wrong — the persistence CLAIM is independently verified by the re-opened-store mark-watered test (which I confirmed is mutation-anchored) — but this is the one spot where the live-evidence narrative is stronger than the artifact supports, and it is exactly the shape a faked 'I reloaded and re-captured' claim would take.
issues.fix: For live-lane screenshots asserting a STATE TRANSITION across an action (reload/navigation), require the post-action capture to be a genuinely fresh frame and assert it is NOT byte-identical to the pre-action frame unless pixel-identity is explicitly expected and noted. Distinguish 'I re-captured and it matched' from 'I reused the prior frame.'

issues.title: Heavy spec-prose doc comments will rot (process-artifact bleed into source)
issues.detail: db.ts is ~280 lines for ~6 small functions, much of it near-identical ~12-line header blocks on markPlantWatered/updatePlantFields/deletePlant restating the same single-transaction + add-vs-put boilerplate, and citing spec sections (migrations §0-§3, R2/R3 lanes, AC numbers) inside production source. season.ts/status.ts carry spec-prose preambles. These will silently diverge the moment the spec moves. The same redundancy appears in the artifacts: the 'tier stays CRITICAL (raise-only)' rationale is re-derived near-verbatim across 6 slice.md + 6 build-verification.md; paperwork ran ~3:1 over code (~7,139 md lines vs 988 src + 1,330 test).
issues.fix: Cut the spec-citation headers to intent-only one-liners; delete the three near-duplicate mutation-function preambles, keeping one short note on the add-vs-put rule and the single-transaction invariant. Replace the re-derived per-slice tier paragraph with a one-line reference to the tiering rule.

issues.title: Duplicate test case pads the 43-count; minor selector-escaping asymmetry
issues.detail: Confirmed: status.test.ts asserts AC-2 'overdue' twice with the IDENTICAL input ('2024-06-10', 5, '2024-06-16') at lines 22-24 and 27-29 — the second comment claims a distinct day-boundary probe but uses the same today value, so it tests nothing new. Separately, cssEscape(id) is applied for edit-form/delete-confirm lookups in main.ts but tests query [data-plant-id='${id}'] with raw unescaped ids; safe today only because ids are crypto.randomUUID, a latent inconsistency if id generation ever changes.
issues.fix: Make the second AC-2 case a genuinely distinct input (e.g. today == next-due+manyDays, or remove it). Mirror cssEscape in the test selectors so the test defends the same id-shape contract the production code does.

recommendations.rec: Fix the front-door id plumbing as the single highest-leverage change: thread initiativeId from grill into build so both run under the same <id>, and have S0 BLOCK if the build <id> dir lacks idea-decisions.md when a domain surface is present. This closes the orphan-able-provenance bug (P1) AND the silent-preflight-floor bug (P1) at once.

Add the In-scope ⊆ AC-covered reconciliation gate: every main-spec In-scope clause must trace to a binary AC, an explicit reused-check acceptance, or a logged waiver before PASS. Apply the P4 reused-check mechanism uniformly so the hemisphere setter could never again slip through unbuilt-yet-PASS. As the concrete first repayment, either ship setHemisphere + a <select> (reusing the already-built getHemisphere/AppSettings) or log an explicit deferral.

Give FAST/FULL something to bite on for storage-backed apps: down-tier single-user, on-device, additive-only IndexedDB persistence to STANDARD/FAST, keeping only durable-shape mutations and schema-store creation at CRITICAL/FULL. This is the single biggest lever on the ~2.5h runtime and directly addresses the everything-CRITICAL pattern all three efficiency-adjacent lenses independently flagged.

Consolidate the live-browser verification: probe browser reachability ONCE early (the capability existed the whole time — the corrupt-chromium block was over-stated and resolved in one S7 pass), then capture once at the phase/initiative gate instead of carrying a per-slice BLOCKED obligation through all 4 phases. Add the state-transition screenshot rule (fresh frame, not byte-identical to the pre-action frame) so the reload-evidence narrative can never outrun the artifact again.

Trim the process-artifact bleed: cut spec-citation doc comments to intent-only one-liners, delete the three near-duplicate db.ts mutation preambles, and replace re-derived per-slice tier rationale with a one-line rule reference. Fix the duplicate AC-2 test and mirror cssEscape in test selectors. None of these touch correctness — they reduce rot surface and the ~3:1 paperwork ratio.

failureScorecard.failure: Mode 1 — env-var excuse for skipping browser tests
failureScorecard.evidence: grep over src/ + tests/ for process.env|import.meta.env|VITE_|NODE_ENV returns ZERO hits (re-confirmed). The browser lane was carried as an explicit BLOCKED obligation per ui-slice, never waved off via an env flag, and resolved for real at S7.

Greenfield build (not even a git repo; all files new). Correctly vacuous, but the grounding discipline that WOULD catch reinvention was still exercised (P1-s01 build.md grounding note populated: capability search verbs×nouns -> zero hits, 'greenfield').

S7 drove a real Playwright + system-Chrome click-through (add -> group -> water -> reload) at localhost:5199, producing 5 env-stamped DEV screenshots (shots/s7-*.png). I opened the before/after-water shots; they depict the exact AC-11 Overdue->Coming-up transition.

The Playwright/chromium failure was real (cached chromium-1200 corrupt -> SIGABRT; P2-s01 also hit a Socket Firewall sudo gate on the CDP port). It was NOT used as a give-up: honestly carried as code-verified at the slice level, then RESOLVED at S7 by switching to channel:'chrome' (system Chrome), a real headed engine.

Env discipline held throughout: TEST ran jsdom + the spec-mandated fake-indexeddb shim (never reconfigured to real storage); the browser ran in DEV with real IndexedDB. No env was switched or mutated to manufacture a green; every slice carries an 'environment-scoped: PASS' row.

IndexedDB is local + reversible, single-user, no live readers, no external migration tooling. createObjectStore appears ONLY inside upgradeSchema keyed on DB_VERSION (db.ts:51-60); the v1->v2 bump is purely additive (new app-settings store, plants untouched). No hand-applied migration step anywhere.

The front door was produced and IS authoritative (main-spec.md:6 cites it; the decision log demonstrably shaped DEV/TEST split, the shim, the IndexedDB-only ruling), so substance HELD. But the PLUMBING is broken: build ran under default id 'initiative' while the front door used 'houseplant-tracker' -> two sibling dirs with NO idea-decisions.md in the build dir, bridged only by a hardcoded path citation. The link survived because the agent went looking, not by mechanism.

final verification.md carries a complete attestation: Verdict PASS / ladderReached R4 / verifiedKind:verified, with a Final Receipt (Shipped / Run-Use / Next), and it self-flags the hemisphere coverage gap rather than hiding it. One caveat noted separately: the after-reload screenshot is byte-identical to after-water (md5 6a454ef...) yet cited as distinct evidence — the attestation exists and is honest overall, but that one live-evidence claim slightly outruns its artifact.

Independently re-verified: I applied the partial-put mutation to markPlantWatered and the suite went 43/43 -> 4 failed | 39 passed (AC-7/8/11 + day-boundary FAIL, unknown-id probe correctly green), restoring to 43/43. No .skip/.only/expect(true)/it.todo in tests/; the lone db.ts .clear() is a NEVER doc-comment, not executable. The fake-indexeddb shim is the designated waived-with-fallback fixture, not a silent stand-in.
